sections_parsed.methods_eligibility:
Concept: AI in dental education addressing competency frameworks, curriculum 
integration, or accreditation. Context: undergraduate, postgraduate, or continuing 
professional development. Date range: 1 January 2018 to 30 April 2026. Language: 
English only.  
Sources of evidence: MEDLINE (via PubMed) searched 30 April 2026; reference 
lists hand searched.  
Charting methods: One reviewer extracted data with 20% verification by a second 
reviewer. Categories: setting, design, sample, AI tool, outcome, findings, limitations, 
Kirkpatrick level.  
Results: Twenty-eight studies were synthesised across five themes: competency 
frameworks, educational needs, educator readiness, curriculum interventions, and 
assessment validity. A global competency framework was published in 2022, but 
implementation remains uneven. Faculty readiness, rather than student interest or 
AI capability, appears to be a recurring constraint: fewer than one third of educators 
reported routine AI use, and emerging evidence links lower perceived readiness to 
poorer student performance. Intervention evidence is mixed: the sole randomised 
trial found skills did not persist when AI support was removed, and the only 

controlled explainable AI experiment revealed automation bias. No intervention 
reached Kirkpatrick Level 3 or 4.
sections_parsed.discussion_conclusions: Conclusions: The literature supports requiring AI literacy at the knowledge level,
sections_parsed.results_critical_appraisal:

integration without staff development risk imposing unsupported obligations. Given 
contextual variation, standards are better framed as outcome-based expectations 
with locally adaptable pathways than as fixed content prescriptions. 
Keywords: Artificial intelligence; dental education; accreditation; curriculum; 
competency framework; scoping review; PRISMA-ScR. 
 
 
 
Introduction 
 
Accreditation in dental education is the external mechanism through which 
professional and educational standards are evaluated, with direct consequences for 
institutional standing, programme recognition, and graduate registration (Field, 
1995; Schönwetter et al., 2026). Over the past decade, many accreditation 
frameworks have begun to incorporate an explicit technology dimension, setting 
expectations about digital environments, digital competencies, and the educational 
technologies institutions should provide, and graduates should demonstrate (Dzyuba 
et al., 2025; Nafea, 2021). This development reflects the normalisation of digital tools 
in clinical dentistry: CAD/CAM, cone-beam imaging, intraoral scanning, electronic 
health records, and teledentistry have shifted from optional adjuncts to expected 
components of practice (Dzyuba et al., 2025). Accreditation has generally followed 
this shift, though often with delay and limited specificity. Standards increasingly 
mention digital competence, yet they frequently remain broad, requiring graduates 
to use contemporary technologies without defining which tools, what level of 
mastery, or how achievement should be assessed (Field, 1995). Artificial intelligence 
presents a more difficult version of this challenge. In dentistry, AI encompasses 
diverse applications including convolutional neural networks (CNN) for image 
interpretation, large language models (LLM) for clinical reasoning support and 
communication, and rule-based decision tools for treatment planning. These systems 
differ in purpose, evidential maturity, and risk profile; standards written for digital 

technology in general are unlikely to be sufficient for AI in particular. The 
educational implications also differ: AI use requires critical appraisal of probabilistic 
outputs, recognition of automation bias, and ethical reasoning under uncertainty, 
rather than operational competence with a digital device. 
This creates what we term the accreditation dilemma: standards must be specific 
enough to be auditable, yet the evidence base for what AI competencies dental 
graduates need, and what educational approaches reliably develop them, remains 
limited and uneven (Schwendicke et al., 2022; Aura-Tormos et al., 2026). Overly 
prescriptive standards risk codifying tools or teaching approaches that the evidence 
does not yet justify, and which may quickly become obsolete; standards that are too 
vague risk becoming symbolic, allowing institutions to appear compliant without 
making meaningful curriculum change. Early conceptual frameworks have proposed 
domains for AI education in dentistry (Schwendicke et al., 2022), and surveys have 
begun to explore student and educator attitudes (Uribe et al., 2024; Kavadella et al., 
2026), while small intervention studies have tested specific tools and teaching 
formats (Glick et al., 2022; Schropp et al., 2024; Caldwell et al., 2026; Kavadella et 
al., 2024). Recent reviews have mapped parts of the field, but none has focused 
specifically on what the literature can justify at the level of accreditation (Aura-
Tormos et al., 2026; Kong et al., 2025; Iniesta and Pérez-Higueras, 2026). That 
distinction matters: evidence that an AI system performs well on a dental task is not 
equivalent to evidence that students should be required to learn that system, or that 
schools should be accredited on that basis (Lafourcade et al., 2025; Ji et al., 2026). 
Capability findings are sometimes used as though they were curricular findings, even 
though the educational and regulatory implications differ. The literature base 
therefore remains fragmented across capability studies, attitude surveys, and small 
educational pilots, with no synthesis organised around the specific question of what 
can and cannot be required through accreditation standards. A comprehensive 
mapping of empirical evidence is necessary to determine which AI competencies are 
defensible for accreditation, what educator and learner readiness looks like, and 
which educational interventions have been tested at which levels of educational 
outcome. 
A scoping review is the appropriate design for this question. Unlike a systematic 
review, which synthesises effect sizes from homogeneous intervention studies, a 
scoping review maps the breadth and nature of evidence across diverse study 

designs, identifying what is known, what remains uncertain, and where conceptual or 
methodological gaps persist (Arksey and O'Malley, 2005; Levac et al., 2010). The 
approach follows the Arksey and O'Malley framework as refined by Levac and 
colleagues, which allows iterative refinement of inclusion criteria and synthesis 
structure in response to the emerging literature, and is reported in accordance with 
updated JBI guidance and PRISMA-ScR (Peters et al., 2020; Tricco et al., 2018). This 
design permits the inclusion of competency frameworks, needs surveys, educator 
readiness studies, controlled interventions, and measurement studies, each of which 
addresses a distinct aspect of whether and how AI should appear in accreditation 
standards, while preserving the evidential distinctions among them. The synthesis is 
therefore organised by the type of evidence each study contributes, reflecting the 
tiered logic by which accreditation bodies and curriculum committees typically 
construct policy: consensus development, feasibility analysis, implementation, and 
evaluation. 
 
 
Methods 
 
Eligibility criteria 
Eligibility criteria were framed using the Population, Concept, Context (PCC) 
framework recommended by JBI for scoping reviews (Peters et al., 2020). 
Inclusion criteria 
• Population: Dental students (undergraduate or postgraduate), dental 
educators, dental faculty, or dental schools. Studies involving dental 
professionals in continuing professional development contexts were included 
where the educational intervention or curriculum development was the focus. 
• Concept: Artificial intelligence in any form, including machine learning, 
deep learning, large language models, generative AI, or AI assisted decision 
support tools, as applied to or integrated within dental education. The concept 
of interest was specifically AI competency frameworks, curriculum 
integration, accreditation alignment, or educational policy related to AI. 
• Context: Undergraduate dental education, postgraduate dental training, or 
continuing professional development in dentistry. Studies had to address 
educational settings rather than purely clinical performance comparisons. 

• Types of sources: Peer reviewed empirical studies (quantitative, qualitative, 
or mixed methods), curriculum framework development papers, competency 
consensus studies, and foundational position papers widely cited by 
accrediting bodies or curriculum committees. Studies were considered 
relevant if they: (a) proposed, evaluated, or directly informed an AI 
competency framework; (b) stated curriculum integration, accreditation 
alignment, or educational policy as an explicit purpose; (c) constituted an 
empirical study of educational needs with explicit curricular 
recommendations; (d) described an educational intervention intended for 
institutional implementation; or (e) constituted a foundational position paper 
directly addressing accreditation or curriculum standards. 
• Date range: Studies published between 1 January 2018 and 30 April 2026. 
This date range was selected to capture the emergence of contemporary AI 
applications in dental education while maintaining currency with recent large 
language model developments. 
• Language: English language publications only, including those with English 
full text versions of non-English original studies. 
Exclusion criteria 
• Large language model performance studies (e.g., LLM performance on 
multiple-choice examinations) that did not propose curricular implications, 
excluded as they assess AI capability rather than educational intervention or 
curriculum need. 
• Single class pilots with fewer than 30 participants and no discussion of 
institutional integration, excluded as they do not provide scalable evidence 
relevant to accreditation standards. 
• AI versus clinician accuracy comparisons without educational framing, 
excluded as they address clinical performance rather than educational 
outcomes. 
• Knowledge, attitude, and practice (KAP) surveys without explicit 
curricular recommendations or with fewer than 100 participants, excluded as 
they do not directly address curriculum or accreditation reform at sufficient 
scale. 

• Bibliometric or scoping reviews that did not directly inform curriculum 
design, excluded as secondary syntheses unless they provided unique 
curriculum framework analysis. 
• Conference abstracts, letters, editorials, and other non-peer 
reviewed material, excluded as they do not present peer reviewed original 
findings subject to methodological appraisal. 
• Non-English language publications without an English full text version, 
excluded due to resource and translation validity constraints. 
Disagreements at the eligibility screening stage were resolved by consensus between 
reviewers, with a third reviewer available to adjudicate if consensus could not be 
reached.
sections_parsed.methods_information_sources:
A single comprehensive search of MEDLINE (via PubMed) was conducted, covering 
publications from 1 January 2018 to 30 April 2026. PubMed was selected for its 
comprehensive coverage of dental and biomedical education research and indexing 
of key journals where relevant literature appears, including the Journal of Dental 
Education, European Journal of Dental Education, International Dental Journal, 
Journal of Dental Research, and Journal of Dentistry (McGowan et al., 2016). Single 
database searches in scoping reviews are methodologically defensible when the 
chosen database aligns with the research aim and the limitation is explicitly 
acknowledged (Levac et al., 2010; Peters et al., 2020). 
Grey literature was deliberately excluded from this review. Conference abstracts, 
dissertations, preprint servers, and unpublished institutional reports were not 
searched, as the review prioritised peer reviewed evidence suitable for informing 
accreditation standards. This decision is acknowledged as a limitation that may have 
excluded emerging institutional innovations not yet reported in indexed journals. 
To strengthen search sensitivity, reference lists of all included studies and three 
comparator reviews were hand searched for additional eligible papers; no further 
studies met the inclusion criteria (Aura-Tormos et al., 2026; Kong et al., 2025; 
Iniesta and Pérez-Higueras, 2026). 
The most recent search was conducted on 30 April 2026.
sections_parsed.methods_data_charting:
The review team iteratively developed a structured data-charting form and pilot-
tested it on five included studies before applying it to the full corpus. The form was 
refined following pilot testing to ensure consistency and completeness of extraction. 
Data were extracted under the following headings: 
• Bibliographic details: First author, year, country, institutional setting 
• Study design: Design classification and sample size 
• Participants: Learner or educator population characteristics 
• Intervention/AI tool and comparator: Type of AI system, comparison 
condition if applicable 
• Outcome measures: Educational outcomes assessed, including Kirkpatrick 
level where applicable 
• Key findings: Quantitative results and main qualitative themes 
• Reported limitations: Study reported methodological constraints 
• Relevance to review question: Explicit connection to accreditation, 
curriculum integration, or competency framework development 
One reviewer performed data extraction, with verification of a random 20% sample 
by a second reviewer. Disagreements were resolved through discussion, with 
recourse to a third reviewer if consensus could not be reached. All extraction 
decisions were logged to maintain an audit trail. 
AI assisted extraction was not used in this review; all charting was performed 
manually by human reviewers. 
Corresponding authors were not contacted for unclear or missing data, as the review 
prioritised mapping the evidence as reported rather than augmenting individual 
studies. 
Each cell in the extraction table was populated directly from the source paper's 
reported findings, with full text retrieval employed where abstracts did not provide 
sufficient detail. This approach enabled comparison of included studies across a 
common set of variables while preserving the evidential differences between 
frameworks, surveys, interventions, and measurement studies.
sections_parsed.results_synthesis:
The synthesis followed JBI scoping review methodology (Peters et al., 2020) and 
emphasised descriptive mapping of evidence rather than inferential synthesis or 
meta-analysis. The approach was informed by Levac et al.'s (2010) recommendation 
that synthesis structure should emerge iteratively from engagement with included 
literature, rather than being imposed a priori. 
The charted corpus was summarised descriptively by frequency counts of studies by 
country, publication year, study design (framework development, survey, 
intervention, measurement study, review), and target population (dental students, 
dental educators, or both). Two reviewers inductively and iteratively coded charted 
extractions to identify convergent themes across studies, with disagreements 
resolved through discussion and recourse to a third reviewer where necessary. 
Themes were organised by the type of evidence each study contributed to the 
accreditation question: curriculum frameworks, needs surveys, educator readiness 
studies, and controlled interventions each address distinct aspects of whether and 
how AI should appear in accreditation standards. 
Studies were grouped into six organisational tiers reflecting their distinct evidential 
roles: (1) curriculum frameworks and foundational position papers; (2) empirical 
evidence on educational needs; (3) educator and faculty readiness; (4) curriculum 
integration interventions; (5) educational measurement and assessment validity; (6) 
comparator reviews. These tiers did not constitute a quality hierarchy but served as a 
presentational device to clarify what each study could contribute to policy formation. 
No formal quality or risk of bias appraisal was undertaken, consistent with JBI 
guidance that scoping reviews prioritise mapping breadth of evidence rather than 
appraising individual study quality (Peters et al., 2020). 
Synthesised findings were mapped back to the research question and the PCC 
framework, identifying which AI competencies are supported by consensus 
(Concept), which populations have been studied (Population), and in which 
educational or regulatory contexts the evidence applies (Context). 
 
 
 
 
 

Results
sections_parsed.results_characteristics:
The PubMed search identified 488 unique records published between 1 January 2018 
and 30 April 2026. Screening against eligibility criteria focused on accreditation and 
curriculum yielded 28 studies for synthesis. Of the 28 included studies, 21 (75%) 
were published in 2024 or later, indicating rapid recent growth. The corpus spans 
thirteen countries plus four multinational studies and includes three curriculum 
frameworks or position papers, six cross sectional surveys, four educator readiness 
studies, seven educational interventions, five educational measurement studies, and 
three comparator reviews (Table 1). The dominant participant populations were 
undergraduate dental students, dental educators and faculty, and postgraduate 
dental residents. The evidence base is weighted towards perceptions, readiness, and 
early implementation rather than long term outcome evaluation. Thematic findings 
across these studies are synthesised in the following subsection.

Synthesis of results 
 
Theme 1: Competency frameworks and curriculum integration 
A global competency framework for AI in dentistry was first published in 2022 
(Schwendicke et al., 2022), establishing four learning outcome domains: terminology 
and machine learning reasoning, use cases and software setup, evaluation metrics 
and impact, and generalisability, accountability, and governance. Islam et al. (2022) 
complemented this by proposing an organisational adoption framework emphasising 
structural, human resource, political, and symbolic dimensions. Schwendicke, Samek 
and Krois (2020) provided foundational justification by highlighting data 
availability, methodological rigour, ethics, and the need for human oversight. 
However, subsequent reviews suggest that integration into curricula has been uneven 
and frequently limited in depth (Aura-Tormos et al., 2026; Kong et al., 2025; Iniesta 
and Pérez-Higueras, 2026). The gap is not the absence of ideas but the absence of 
consistent translation from consensus into practice. 
 
Theme 2: Educational needs and learner readiness 
Six studies provided empirical evidence on learner and educator needs. Kavadella et 
al. (2026) surveyed 508 undergraduate students across eight countries; although 
over three quarters reported a broad understanding of AI, just over one third were 
familiar with its applications in dentistry, and more than half considered their 
existing training insufficient for clinical AI use. Ellakany et al. (2025) found that 
prior AI use varied by educational level and specialty, with graduates reporting 
greater use in prosthodontics, restorative dentistry, and implantology, while 
undergraduates reported more use in treatment planning and selected specialties. 
Pauwels and Del Rey (2021) reported that a single one-hour introductory lecture 
increased agreement about AI's future role and reduced concern that AI might 

replace radiologists. Alkahtany et al. (2026) found that among Saudi dental students, 
roughly two thirds indicated awareness of AI in general, yet fewer than 13% had 
applied it clinically and only 38% had received any formal instruction. Suri et al. 
(2026) showed that after exposure to Overjet Caries Assist, most students believed AI 
improved detection and interpretation, but only 16.7% felt their curriculum had 
prepared them for clinical use; that study also identified a need for structured faculty 
training, earlier exposure, and explicit attention to automation bias. Uribe et al. 
(2024) surveyed 428 educators worldwide and found that whilst nearly one third 
reported current AI use, more than half expressed apprehension about diminished 
interpersonal contact, with the lack of explicit protocols emerging as the 
predominant worry.

sections_parsed.discussion_summary:
This review identifies a literature base that is growing rapidly but remains uneven in 
depth and maturity. The objective was to map the empirical evidence to determine 
what this evidence can and cannot support at the level of accreditation requirements. 
Four principal findings dominate the evidence base: first, a credible international 
curriculum framework exists but implementation remains fragmented; second, 
faculty readiness rather than student interest or AI capability constrains 
implementation; third, intervention evidence is mixed, with the sole RCT failing to 
show skill retention and the only explainable AI experiment suggesting automation 
bias; fourth, no included intervention reached Kirkpatrick Level 3 or 4, meaning the 
evidence base speaks to reactions and short term learning but not to sustained 
behaviour or patient outcomes. 
For educators, the evidence suggests that AI literacy at the knowledge level, 
encompassing critical appraisal of AI outputs, recognition and mitigation of 
automation bias, and ethical frameworks, can be justified but applied competencies 
remain aspirational pending stronger intervention evidence. Students may value AI 
tools, yet they also perceive a gap between the technology they encounter and the 
curriculum that prepares them to use it (Suri et al., 2026). For researchers, the 
evidence base reveals substantial gaps: no study assessed behaviour change in 
clinical practice or patient level outcomes; most interventions were single institution 
pilots with short follow up; controlled designs were rare, and when employed 
(Schropp et al., 2024; Glick et al., 2022) they revealed limitations rather than 
confirming benefits. Multi institution, longitudinal, and comparator group studies 
are urgently needed, as are studies assessing not whether students react positively to 
AI but whether they deploy it safely, critically, and effectively in authentic clinical 
settings. For accreditors and policymakers, the current evidence supports requiring 
foundational AI literacy, terminology, evaluation metrics, ethical reasoning, and bias 
mitigation, while treating applied clinical competencies as recommended rather than 
mandatory. Evidence that an AI system performs well on a dental task does not 
constitute evidence that students should be required to learn that system, or that 
schools should be accredited on that basis (Lafourcade et al., 2025; Ji et al., 2026). 
Accreditation standards that compel AI integration without parallel investment in 

staff development risk imposing an unsupported obligation, as readiness studies 
consistently indicate that routine AI use among teaching staff remains limited and 
confidence is uneven (Uribe et al., 2024; Leung et al., 2026; Abdullah et al., 2025). 
The distinction between literacy and competence is central: schools can reasonably 
be expected to teach students to interrogate AI outputs, identify limitations, and 
resist blind reliance, but cannot yet be held accountable for guaranteeing that 
graduates will deploy AI safely and effectively across all clinical contexts as a formal 
graduate competency. 
The shape of the evidence base itself warrants reflection. Geographically, the corpus 
is concentrated in high income Anglophone and European settings, with 75% of 
studies published in 2024 or later, indicating both rapid recent growth and limited 
temporal depth. Study designs are weighted toward cross-sectional surveys and 
single institution pilots; only one randomised controlled trial was identified, and no 
study assessed workplace behaviour change or patient outcomes. This distribution 
reflects an evidence base at an early stage of maturity: consensus frameworks have 
been proposed, needs have been surveyed, and small-scale feasibility has been 
demonstrated, but robust experimental evidence and higher order outcomes remain 
absent. The predominance of Level 1 and Level 2 Kirkpatrick, outcomes reaction and 
learning, over Level 3 and Level 4 outcomes, behaviour and results, is consistent with 
this developmental trajectory but limits the confidence with which findings can 
inform regulatory standards. Contextual variation across the included studies is 
substantial: awareness, institutional readiness, and implementation feasibility differ 
markedly by country, institution, and user group. This heterogeneity does not 
invalidate the synthesis, but it suggests that accreditation standards function more 
effectively when written as outcomes and principles rather than rigid curricular 
inventories, permitting local adaptation while maintaining accountability for core 
competencies. The evidence currently supports a tiered approach: requiring AI 
literacy, ethical awareness, and critical appraisal as mandatory; recommending 
applied competencies as aspirational; and mandating institutional evidence of faculty 
preparation and curricular mechanisms to support implementation.
sections_parsed.discussion_limitations:
Several limitations of this scoping review should be acknowledged. 
Database coverage: The search was restricted to a single database (MEDLINE via 
PubMed). While PubMed provides comprehensive coverage of dental and biomedical 
education research, additional databases such as ERIC, Embase, Web of Science, and 
the Cochrane Library may have yielded further relevant studies, particularly 
educational research not indexed in MEDLINE. This choice prioritised precision and 
manageability over exhaustive retrieval and is consistent with pragmatic scoping 
review approaches, but it may have excluded relevant literature. 
Grey literature exclusion: Conference abstracts, dissertations, preprint servers, 
and unpublished institutional reports were deliberately excluded. This decision 
focused the review on peer reviewed evidence suitable for informing accreditation 
standards, but it may have missed emerging institutional innovations, pilot projects, 
or policy documents not yet disseminated through indexed journals. 
Language restriction: Only English language publications were included. This 
restriction likely excluded relevant literature published in languages such as Spanish, 
Portuguese, German, French, Chinese, and Arabic, potentially underrepresenting AI 
integration efforts in non-Anglophone dental education systems and limiting the 
geographic representativeness of the synthesis. 
Single reviewer screening and charting: Screening and data charting were 
conducted by a single reviewer, with verification of a subset by a second reviewer. 
While all decisions were logged to maintain an audit trail, dual independent 
screening and charting would have strengthened the reliability of study selection and 
data extraction, reducing the risk of inadvertent exclusion or charting error. 
Absence of formal critical appraisal: No formal quality or risk of bias appraisal 
was undertaken. This aligns with JBI scoping review guidance, which prioritises 
mapping breadth over appraising individual study quality, but it limits the ability to 
distinguish higher quality evidence from lower quality evidence when drawing 
implications for practice or policy. 
These limitations do not invalidate the mapping function of this scoping review, 
which was to identify what types of evidence currently inform AI competency 
frameworks, curriculum integration, and accreditation standards in dental 
education, and to characterise the scope and distribution of that evidence base. 
 

 
 
Conclusion 
 
This scoping review mapped the current evidence on artificial intelligence in dental 
education and its implications for accreditation standards across 28 included studies, 
identifying five principal themes: competency frameworks and curriculum 
integration, educational needs and learner readiness, educator and faculty readiness, 
curriculum integration interventions, and educational measurement and assessment 
validity. 
Summary of findings: A credible international competency framework for AI in 
dentistry exists, establishing four learning outcome domains encompassing 
terminology, use cases, evaluation metrics, and governance. However, 
implementation into curricula has been uneven and frequently limited in depth. 
Student awareness of AI in general is widespread, yet familiarity with dental AI 
applications and confidence in clinical use remain low, with more than half of 
surveyed students considering their existing training insufficient. Faculty readiness 
emerges as the principal constraint on implementation: across international surveys, 
fewer than one third of educators reported routine AI use, and the majority 
expressed concern about the lack of explicit protocols and training. Intervention 
evidence is mixed. The sole randomised controlled trial found that skills acquired 
during AI assisted training did not persist when support was removed, and the only 
controlled experiment involving explainable AI revealed evidence of overreliance on 
machine suggestions. Favourable intervention outcomes were constrained by 
industry authorship, single institution recruitment, possible novelty bias, or 
dependence on self-reported rather than objectively measured endpoints. No 
included intervention assessed behaviour change in clinical practice or patient level 
outcomes; the evidence base speaks to reactions and short-term learning but not to 
sustained competence or results. 
Implications for practice: Programme leads might consider introducing AI 
literacy at the knowledge level, encompassing terminology, evaluation metrics, and 
the interpretation of probabilistic outputs, as a foundational component of the 
curriculum, recognising that applied clinical competencies remain aspirational 
pending stronger intervention evidence. Early exposure to AI concepts, integrated 

with existing courses in research methods, evidence-based practice, or digital 
dentistry, may address the gap students perceive between the technology they 
encounter and the curriculum that prepares them to use it. Explicit attention to the 
limitations of AI systems, including the tendency to over rely on machine generated 
recommendations, warrants inclusion in clinical reasoning and diagnostic curricula. 
Faculty development emerges as a prerequisite for effective implementation: without 
institutional investment in staff training, curricular AI content risks becoming 
symbolic rather than transformative. 
Implications for policy and accreditation: Accreditation bodies may wish to 
consider requiring foundational AI literacy, including critical appraisal of AI outputs, 
recognition of system limitations, and ethical reasoning under uncertainty, while 
treating applied clinical competencies as recommended rather than mandatory until 
stronger evidence of durable skill transfer and workplace behaviour change becomes 
available. Standards that compel AI integration without parallel expectations for 
faculty development risk imposing obligations that institutions cannot yet meet. 
Given the substantial contextual variation observed across the included studies in 
awareness, institutional readiness, and implementation feasibility, accreditation 
standards may function more effectively when framed as outcome-based 
expectations with locally adaptable implementation pathways, rather than as 
prescriptive lists of tools, platforms, or fixed curricular content. Evidence that an AI 
system performs well on a dental task does not constitute evidence that students 
should be required to learn that system or that schools should be accredited on that 
basis; capability findings and curricular findings address distinct regulatory 
questions. 
Implications for future research: The evidence base would benefit from 
controlled intervention studies with longer follow up periods, assessing whether AI 
assisted training improves diagnostic or clinical decision-making performance when 
measured weeks or months after the intervention and in the absence of continued AI 
support. Studies evaluating the effect of explainable AI on calibration, whether 
learners appropriately adjust their reliance on machine recommendations based on 
contextual cues and their own expertise, would address the automation bias findings 
from the sole controlled experiment in this corpus. Multi institution collaborations 
are needed to test the generalisability of single site pilot findings and to identify 
which contextual factors moderate intervention success. Longitudinal cohort studies 

tracking graduates' actual use of AI tools in clinical practice and linking that use to 
patient outcomes or practice efficiency, would provide the Kirkpatrick Level 3 and 
Level 4 evidence currently absent from the literature. Finally, implementation 
science studies examining how schools successfully integrate faculty development, 
curricular reform, and infrastructure investment would offer practical guidance for 
the translation gap identified across frameworks, needs surveys, and readiness 
studies. 
This review contributes to the health professions education literature by 
distinguishing the types of evidence available to inform AI related accreditation 
standards, and by clarifying the boundaries between what current evidence can 
justify and what remains aspirational, thereby supporting proportionate and 
evidence informed policy development in dental education.

2. Open with a single sentence in the form: 'Across the N included studies, [X] major themes emerged: theme A, theme B, theme C, …' giving an exact study count and naming each theme.

3. Create H3 subsection headings for each of the 3–6 themes. For each theme provide: (a) a one-sentence definition; (b) a list of contributing studies with (Author et al., Year) citations drawn from the charted corpus; (c) a statement on whether findings converge or diverge, drawing on the `findings.effect_direction` field; (d) one sentence on methodological caveats (e.g. small samples, single-institution surveys).

4. Remove the inline numbered list of 'six organisational tiers' and instead develop these as the H3 subsections (if they are indeed the themes) — but ensure each is populated with charted findings, not left as a label.

5. Add a closing paragraph that explicitly maps themes to the research question: state which PCC sub-questions are well covered by the evidence and which remain underexplored. Do NOT make recommendations.
6. If the corpus contains fewer than 10 studies, add a sentence stating this and framing the synthesis as a preliminary mapping.
7. Expand the section to 550–1100 words. The current 288 words cannot support the required structure.
8. Remove methodological-justification sentences (e.g. 'No formal quality or risk of bias appraisal was undertaken…') — these belong in Methods, not in Item 18 results.
strengths: The draft correctly avoids making recommendations for practice or future research.
The intent to map findings back to the PCC framework is signalled, even if not executed.

revision_notes: Remove the entire 'Synthesis of results' block (Themes 1–3). That content belongs in the Item 18 section (results_synthesis) and must not appear in results_characteristics. Doing so will also bring the section close to the 280–600 word target.

Reformat Table 1 as a valid markdown table using pipes and a dash-separator row. Example header line: `| First author (year) | Country | Design | Population (N) | Intervention / focus | Key findings (short) |` followed by `|---|---|---|---|---|---|` and then one row per study. The current layout will not render in Streamlit or Word.

Confirm every author-year row in Table 1 maps to a study with `inclusion_recommendation == 'include'` in the project corpus. The reviewer was supplied with no charted corpus, so as the rubric stands every citation here is unverifiable; supply the chart file or remove unverifiable rows.

Tighten the opening paragraph: it currently includes the search yield ('488 unique records') and screening outcome, which belong in the PRISMA flow / Item 14 (selection of sources), not Item 15. Replace with a clean corpus-level summary (n, year range, country counts, design counts, population counts) only.
Audit cell word counts: a few cells (e.g., Schwendicke 2022 Key findings, Suri 2026 Key findings) are at or near 15 words — trim to ensure compliance.

Check the year range claim. The opening says 'published between 1 January 2018 and 30 April 2026' but the earliest included row is Schwendicke (2020). State the actual year range of the *included* corpus (2020–2026), not the search window.

Add a single forward-pointing closing sentence after the table (the current one sits before the table). Move it to immediately follow Table 1 so the reader transitions cleanly into the synthesis subsection.

Standardise the Country column: 'International', 'Multinational', 'Worldwide', 'Multi country' all appear and likely conflate distinct chart values. Use the exact charted term, or define a single label (e.g., 'Multinational') and apply it consistently.
strengths: Opening paragraph attempts the required corpus-level descriptors (n, year span, country count, design mix, populations).
Table content is comprehensive in coverage, with one row per declared included study and use of 'NR' where data are absent.
Tone is academic and neutral, and UK English is largely maintained in the prose.

requirement_checks.evidence: Paragraph 2 addresses educators, researchers, and accreditors/policymakers in turn with explicit signposting.

requirement_checks.evidence: Paragraph 3 does describe the shape (geography, designs, year span, Kirkpatrick levels) but then drifts into prescriptive recommendations ('evidence currently supports a tiered approach: requiring... recommending... mandating...'), which belongs in conclusions.

revision_notes: Remove all eight author-year citations. A Summary of Evidence section synthesises themes; individual study attribution belongs in the Results section. If a finding must be anchored, refer to it as 'the sole RCT' or 'the explainable-AI experiment' without re-citing.

Rewrite paragraph 1 so it does not enumerate findings as 'first... second... third... fourth...'. The spec explicitly forbids theme-by-theme re-listing. Instead, synthesise the cross-cutting message in 4–6 flowing sentences (e.g. that a coherent framework exists but is undermined by faculty readiness gaps, with intervention evidence confined to lower Kirkpatrick levels).

Delete the prescriptive sentence in paragraph 2: 'Multi institution, longitudinal, and comparator group studies are urgently needed...'. Recast as a descriptive evidence-gap statement (e.g. 'The evidence base contains no multi-institution longitudinal studies and no studies assessing clinical behaviour or patient outcomes.'). Recommendations belong in discussion_conclusions (Item 21).

Delete or radically soften the closing recommendation block in paragraph 3 ('The evidence currently supports a tiered approach: requiring... recommending... mandating...'). This is a concrete policy recommendation and belongs in discussion_conclusions, not in the Summary of Evidence.

Remove evaluative/speculative phrases such as 'risk imposing an unsupported obligation' and 'cannot yet be held accountable for guaranteeing...'. These exceed the interpretive remit of Item 19. Restate as what the evidence does and does not currently demonstrate.

Tighten the accreditor-facing paragraph so it maps findings to audience implications without prescribing standards. For example, 'The evidence base is sufficient to characterise foundational AI literacy needs but is insufficient to specify applied clinical competencies' is descriptive; 'accreditors should require literacy and recommend applied competencies' is prescriptive — keep the former.

In paragraph 3, retain the genuinely strong content on geography, design distribution, year span, and Kirkpatrick level distribution, but end the paragraph on an interpretive note about what this shape means for confidence in the findings, rather than on a tiered policy recommendation.
strengths: The audience-mapping structure in paragraph 2 (educators, researchers, accreditors) is clearly signposted and addresses the spec's second structural requirement directly.
The reflection on evidence-base shape in paragraph 3 — geography, design weighting, Kirkpatrick level distribution, single RCT — is exactly the type of meta-level observation Item 19 calls for.
The literacy-versus-competence distinction is a genuinely useful interpretive frame that synthesises across themes rather than re-listing them.
Word count sits comfortably within the 380–700 target.
UK English and academic register are maintained consistently.

requirement_checks.requirement: An opening sentence framing this as the review team's own reflection on the limitations of the review process.
requirement_checks.evidence: Opens with 'Several limitations of this scoping review should be acknowledged.'

requirement_checks.requirement: A bullet list of 3–6 specific limitations, each with a one-sentence explanation.
requirement_checks.evidence: Five limitations are present and labelled by category, but they are not formatted as a markdown bullet list, and each item runs to multiple sentences rather than a single one-sentence explanation.

requirement_checks.requirement: A closing sentence acknowledging that these limitations constrain the conclusions but do not invalidate the mapping function the review is designed to perform.
requirement_checks.evidence: Final paragraph of the limitations section explicitly states limitations 'do not invalidate the mapping function of this scoping review'.

requirement_checks.requirement: Word target 280–500 words.
requirement_checks.evidence: Section is 1281 words — more than 2.5× the upper bound. Even the limitations portion alone (before the Conclusion) exceeds the cap.

requirement_checks.requirement: Do NOT use this section to discuss limitations of included studies or of the evidence base as a whole.
requirement_checks.evidence: The limitations portion correctly focuses on review process choices (database, grey literature, language, single reviewer, no appraisal).

requirement_checks.requirement: Scope guardrail — section is Item 20 (Limitations) only.
requirement_checks.evidence: The draft includes an entire Conclusion section with Summary of findings, Implications for practice, Implications for policy and accreditation, Implications for future research, and a reference list. These belong in discussion_summary (Item 19), discussion_conclusions (Item 22), and the references section — not in Item 20.

revision_notes: Delete everything after the closing sentence of the limitations paragraph. The 'Conclusion', 'Summary of findings', 'Implications for practice', 'Implications for policy and accreditation', 'Implications for future research', and reference list do not belong in Item 20 and must be moved to their correct PRISMA-ScR sections (Items 19 and 22).
Reformat the five limitations as a true markdown bullet list using '-' or '*' markers, not bold labels followed by paragraph prose.

Compress each bullet to a single sentence containing the limitation and a brief consequence (e.g. 'Single-database search: restricting retrieval to MEDLINE/PubMed prioritised precision but may have excluded education-specific literature indexed in ERIC, Embase, or Web of Science.'). Remove justificatory tangents such as the 'consistent with pragmatic scoping review approaches' aside.
Reduce total section length from 1281 words to between 280 and 500 words. The opening sentence, 3–6 single-sentence bullets, and a one-sentence closing should comfortably fit this envelope.

Retain the five categories chosen (database coverage, grey literature, language, single reviewer, absence of formal appraisal) — they are appropriate and specific — but trim them; do not add new categories.
Ensure no author-year or numbered citations appear in this section; the current draft includes a full reference list which must be removed entirely from Item 20.
strengths: The five limitations selected are appropriately specific to the review process (not the evidence base or individual studies) and align with the suggested categories in the specification.
The language restriction bullet is exemplary in naming concrete excluded literatures (Spanish, Portuguese, Chinese, Arabic) rather than generic hedging.
The closing sentence correctly reframes the limitations as constraining but not invalidating the mapping function of the review.

originality_report.overall_summary: The manuscript presents a scoping review of AI in dental education with a distinctive policy framing oriented to accreditation standards, which appears to be its principal novel contribution. The empirical synthesis draws heavily on existing frameworks (notably Schwendicke 2022) and overlaps thematically with cited comparator reviews (Aura-Tormos 2026; Kong 2025; Iniesta 2026), but the manuscript explicitly differentiates itself from these. No reference corpus was supplied for direct comparison, so overlap assessment is based solely on the manuscript's own citations and internal positioning.

section_reports.summary: Rationale frames a specific accreditation-policy gap not addressed by prior reviews, which is a defensible novel angle, though the underlying premise (fragmented evidence on AI in dental education) is well-rehearsed.
unique_contributions: Explicit framing of an 'accreditation dilemma' distinguishing capability evidence from curricular/regulatory evidence

section_reports.summary: Objective is narrowly scoped to what evidence can support at accreditation level, differentiating it from broader mapping reviews.
unique_contributions: Accreditation-specific evidentiary objective rather than general mapping

section_reports.summary: Standard PCC/JBI eligibility approach; procedural resemblance to other scoping reviews is expected and not overlap. Specific exclusion criteria (e.g., LLM performance studies without curricular implication) reflect the accreditation framing.
unique_contributions: Exclusion logic tailored to distinguish capability vs curricular evidence

section_reports.summary: Single-database MEDLINE search is a methodological limitation rather than an overlap concern. No substantive duplication detected.
Standard JBI-aligned charting procedure. Procedural similarity to other scoping reviews is expected and not flagged.
unique_contributions: Charting variable 'relevance to accreditation/competency framework' explicitly included

section_reports.summary: Six-tier evidential organisation mapped to policy formation logic is a modest organisational novelty. Thematic categories themselves resemble those used in cited comparator reviews.
unique_contributions: Six-tier evidential mapping aligned to accreditation policy logic

section_reports.summary: Descriptive characterisation of 28 included studies is an original empirical contribution of this review.
unique_contributions: Original characterisation table of 28 studies with Kirkpatrick mapping

section_reports.summary: Mixed introductory/conclusory content; the substantive observations about uneven implementation echo cited reviews (Aura-Tormos 2026; Kong 2025; Iniesta 2026) but are appropriately attributed.

overlap_matches.overlap_description: The four-domain AI curriculum framework is the structural backbone of the synthesis; manuscript restates these domains repeatedly but cites the original appropriately.

section_reports.summary: Synthesises findings into a tiered accreditation recommendation (mandatory literacy, recommended applied competencies, required faculty development evidence). This tiered policy recommendation is the manuscript's clearest novel contribution.

overlap_matches.reference_label: Schwendicke et al., 2022; Uribe et al., 2024; Leung et al., 2026
overlap_matches.overlap_description: Observations about faculty readiness gaps and uneven implementation closely track findings already established by these cited works; manuscript appropriately attributes them.

unique_contributions: Tiered accreditation recommendation distinguishing mandatory literacy from aspirational applied competence
Explicit linkage of automation-bias and skill-retention findings to regulatory caution

section_reports.summary: Standard limitations enumeration; no novelty concern and no overlap concern.
Conclusion restates the tiered accreditation framing. Substantive policy recommendation is the manuscript's distinctive contribution; the underlying empirical claims are drawn from cited literature.
unique_contributions: Outcome-based accreditation framing with locally adaptable implementation pathways
